title: Motion & Interaction Design Principles
description: Animation and interaction design principles, patterns, and techniques for UI, with concrete timing and accessibility guidance.

entries.name: Disney's 12 principles of animation (applied to UI)
entries.summary: The twelve classic animation principles (squash & stretch, anticipation, staging, timing, easing/slow-in-slow-out, follow-through, etc.) adapted as a vocabulary for interface motion.
entries.significance: Provides a shared, decades-tested language for making UI motion feel physical and intentional rather than arbitrary; championed for the web by Val Head.
key_facts: Originated with Disney animators Ollie Johnston and Frank Thomas in 'The Illusion of Life' (1981); comprises 12 principles.
Anticipation in UI foreshadows an action (e.g. a button reacting on hover before the change).
Staging directs the eye to what matters and keeps non-essential motion minimal.
Slow-in/slow-out corresponds to easing: Val Head argues limiting yourself to only ease-in/ease-out caps expressiveness versus custom cubic-beziers.
examples: Staged reveal drawing focus to a new panel

sources.title: What Does Disney Know About Interface Animation Anyway?
UI Animation: How to Apply Disney's 12 Principles of Animation to UI Design

entries.name: Easing (timing functions)
entries.summary: Easing controls how an animation's rate changes over time. Standard curves are ease-in (slow start), ease-out (slow end), ease-in-out, and linear.
entries.significance: Easing is what makes motion feel natural rather than mechanical; choosing the right curve communicates weight, causality, and polish.
key_facts: Ease-out (decelerate) suits elements entering the screen so they arrive gently under the user's eye.
Ease-in (accelerate) suits elements leaving/exiting because they need less attention.
Linear timing looks mechanical and is best reserved for continuous motion like spinners or moving color/opacity, not spatial movement.
Material's standard easing set defines emphasized, standard, and legacy accelerate/decelerate curves for consistent feel.

entries.name: cubic-bezier() custom easing
entries.summary: A CSS easing function defined by two control points, cubic-bezier(x1,y1,x2,y2), letting designers author custom acceleration curves beyond the named keywords.
entries.significance: Unlocks brand-specific, expressive motion; the named keywords are just presets of specific cubic-bezier curves.
key_facts: The named CSS keywords map to fixed curves: ease is cubic-bezier(0.25, 0.1, 0.25, 1); linear is cubic-bezier(0, 0, 1, 1).
x values (time) must stay within 0-1, but y values may exceed the range to create overshoot/anticipation ('bounce back').
Custom curves let a whole product share a distinctive motion signature rather than defaulting to ease.
CSS also offers steps() and, more recently, linear() for approximating springs and complex curves.
examples: Brand-tuned easing across a design system

entries.name: Spring physics animation
entries.summary: Physics-based motion driven by stiffness, damping, and mass instead of a fixed duration and curve, producing naturally settling, velocity-aware movement.
entries.significance: Springs feel more organic and interruptible than duration-based tweens, and carry gesture velocity into the animation; core to Framer Motion / Motion and iOS animation.
key_facts: Configured by stiffness (higher = snappier), damping (higher = less oscillation/bounce), and mass (higher = more sluggish).
Motion (Framer Motion) defaults are stiffness 100, damping 10, mass 1.
If damping is 0 the spring oscillates indefinitely; damping dissipates energy until it settles.
Springs incorporate the velocity of an in-progress gesture, giving continuous, interruptible feedback that fixed-duration tweens cannot.

entries.name: Motion duration guidelines
entries.summary: Guidance on how long UI transitions should last, scaled to the size of the moving element and the platform.
entries.significance: Duration is the single most impactful motion parameter for whether an interface feels responsive or sluggish; Material publishes concrete ranges.
key_facts: Small transitions (icons, selection controls) are short: around 100ms; a switch toggle is about 100ms.
Most mobile transitions occur around 300ms; complex full-screen transitions may run up to ~375ms.
Transitions exceeding 400ms tend to feel too slow.
Exit/dismiss transitions use shorter durations than enter transitions; tablet durations run ~30% longer than mobile, desktop often shorter.

entries.name: Microinteractions (Saffer's model)
entries.summary: Small, single-purpose interaction moments (toggling a setting, liking a post) that Dan Saffer models as four parts: trigger, rules, feedback, and loops & modes.
entries.significance: Microinteractions are where a product's craft and personality show; Saffer's 2013 book made them a first-class design concern.
key_facts: Trigger: what initiates the microinteraction, either user-initiated (tap/click) or system-initiated (a condition is met).
Rules: the logic determining what happens once triggered.
Feedback: what the user perceives about the rules, via visual cue, sound, or haptic.
Loops & modes: the duration/repetition of the interaction and any change to normal behavior (e.g. a 'do not disturb' mode).

entries.name: Hover states & interactive feedback
entries.summary: Visual changes on pointer hover (and focus) that signal an element is interactive and reacts before the user commits.
entries.significance: Hover feedback communicates affordance and anticipation, a key confidence-builder on pointer devices; it must have non-hover equivalents for touch/keyboard.
key_facts: Hover is an anticipation cue: reacting on hover tells users an element is interactive before they click.
Hover does not exist on touch, so critical information must never be hover-only; provide focus and touch equivalents.
Pair hover with a visible :focus-visible state so keyboard users get equivalent feedback.
Keep hover transitions fast (roughly 100-200ms) so the interface feels immediately responsive.
examples: Link underline on hover and focus

sources.title: What Does Disney Know About Interface Animation Anyway?

entries.name: Loading & progress animation
entries.summary: Motion shown while the system works, ranging from indeterminate spinners to determinate progress and content-shaped skeletons.
entries.significance: Loading feedback keeps users engaged during unavoidable waits and reduces the sense of a frozen or broken interface.
key_facts: Provide feedback within the Doherty ~400ms window so users know the system responded.
Use a determinate progress indicator when completion can be estimated; indeterminate (spinner) when it cannot.
For content that will fill a known layout, skeleton screens are perceived as faster than spinners.
Animation buys patience: a visually engaging wait feels shorter than a static or blank one.

sources.title: Everything you need to know about skeleton screens

entries.name: Skeleton screens (motion)
entries.summary: Animated gray placeholders shaped like incoming content, often with a subtle shimmer, shown during load to preview the forthcoming layout.
entries.significance: A perceived-performance technique: by anticipating layout, skeletons make waits feel shorter than spinners even at identical load times.
key_facts: Coined/popularized by Luke Wroblewski in 2013 to reduce perceived wait time.
The shimmer/pulse animation should be subtle and respect prefers-reduced-motion.
Skeleton shapes must match the real content to avoid a jarring layout shift when data arrives.
Gradually revealing content shifts attention to the content forming rather than to the wait itself.

sources.title: Everything you need to know about skeleton screens

entries.name: Page / view transitions
entries.summary: Animated changes between screens or views that preserve spatial continuity and context as the user navigates.
entries.significance: Well-designed transitions reduce disorientation by showing how one view relates to the next, replacing abrupt cuts.
key_facts: Transitions should express the relationship/direction of navigation (forward, back, hierarchy) to preserve a sense of place.
Keep them within duration budgets (mobile ~300ms; large full-screen up to ~375ms) so navigation stays snappy.
The browser View Transitions API enables animated DOM state changes, including cross-document transitions.
Always honor prefers-reduced-motion by substituting a simple cross-fade or no motion.

entries.name: Shared element transition
entries.summary: A transition where an element visually persists and morphs from its position in one view to its position in the next, creating a continuous link.
entries.significance: The strongest continuity cue in UI motion: it directly connects a source (e.g. a thumbnail) to its destination (a detail image), so users never lose the thread.
key_facts: A shared element animates position, size, and shape from source to target rather than fading in a new element.
It is a Material 'container transform' style pattern and is expressible via the View Transitions API's named elements.
The shared element must be identifiable across both states (e.g. matching view-transition-name) to animate correctly.
Because it involves prominent motion, provide a reduced-motion fallback (simple fade/cut).
examples: Photo thumbnail expanding into full-screen detail
List item morphing into its detail page

entries.name: Parallax scrolling
entries.summary: An effect where background and foreground layers move at different speeds during scroll, creating an illusion of depth.
entries.significance: A popular storytelling and branding device, but a common accessibility hazard and performance pitfall when overused.
key_facts: Parallax is motion that can trigger dizziness/nausea in users with vestibular disorders; it must respect prefers-reduced-motion.
Large, screen-spanning motion is the highest-risk kind; reduce or disable it under reduced-motion.
By age 40, over 35% of adults have experienced some vestibular dysfunction, so motion sensitivity is common.
Modern CSS scroll-driven animations can implement parallax off the main thread for better performance than JS scroll listeners.

entries.name: Scroll-triggered animation
entries.summary: Animations that fire when an element enters the viewport as the user scrolls, commonly fade/slide-in reveals.
entries.significance: Guides attention and adds rhythm to long pages, but can harm reading if it delays or hides content.
key_facts: Classic implementations use IntersectionObserver to trigger reveals when elements scroll into view.
Avoid making content invisible until it animates in, since failures or reduced-motion can leave content hidden.
Respect prefers-reduced-motion by showing content immediately without the entrance animation.
Newer CSS view-progress timelines can drive these reveals declaratively and off the main thread.
examples: Staggered reveal of a feature grid

sources.title: Animate elements on scroll with Scroll-driven animations

entries.name: Staggered animation
entries.summary: Animating a group of elements with small sequential delays so they enter one after another rather than all at once.
entries.significance: Creates a sense of choreography and hierarchy, guiding the eye through a set of items in order and feeling more crafted than a simultaneous reveal.
key_facts: Each item's animation starts a small offset after the previous (e.g. 20-60ms per item), producing a cascade.
Total stagger time must stay bounded so later items don't feel slow; cap the number of staggered items or the per-item delay.
Motion (Framer Motion) supports this directly via staggerChildren on a parent variant.
Respect prefers-reduced-motion by showing the group at once without the cascade.

entries.name: Morphing / shape transformation
entries.summary: Animating one shape or element smoothly into another, such as a hamburger icon morphing into a close (x), or a card expanding into a full view.
entries.significance: Reinforces that two states are the same object in different modes, preserving object continuity and clarifying cause and effect.
key_facts: Morphing communicates that an element transformed rather than being replaced, maintaining object permanence.
Performant morphs animate transform and opacity (GPU-friendly) rather than layout properties like width/height/top/left.
The FLIP technique and libraries like GSAP Flip animate between measured start/end states for smooth layout morphs.
Keep morph durations within motion budgets and provide a reduced-motion fallback.

sources.title: Animating Layouts with the FLIP Technique

entries.name: Gesture-driven interaction
entries.summary: Interactions driven by touch/pointer gestures, swipe, drag, pinch, pull, where motion tracks the finger and responds to velocity.
entries.significance: Central to mobile UX; direct manipulation where content follows the finger feels tangible, but hidden gestures harm discoverability and accessibility.
key_facts: Direct-manipulation gestures should track the pointer 1:1 and carry release velocity into a settling spring for a natural feel.
Gesture-only actions are inaccessible on their own; always provide a visible, operable alternative (button/control).
Provide affordances (drag handles, peeking content) so users can discover available gestures.
Handle interruption: users should be able to grab and redirect an in-flight gesture animation.

entries.name: Haptic feedback
entries.summary: Tactile feedback (vibration/taptic) that accompanies an interaction to confirm it physically, one of Saffer's feedback channels.
entries.significance: Adds a non-visual, non-auditory confirmation that strengthens microinteractions and accessibility, especially on mobile.
key_facts: Haptics are one of the feedback modalities in Saffer's microinteraction model, alongside visual and sound.
On the web, the Vibration API can trigger simple device vibration, though support and control are limited versus native taptic engines.
Use haptics sparingly and meaningfully (confirm success, signal a boundary); overuse desensitizes and annoys.
Respect user/system settings that disable vibration, and never rely on haptics as the only feedback.
examples: Vibration on reaching a scroll boundary

entries.name: Doherty threshold (400ms)
entries.summary: A UX law stating that interaction stays productive and engaging when system response stays under ~400ms, so the user never shifts from doing to waiting.
entries.significance: Sets a concrete responsiveness target that underpins both real performance work and perceived-performance techniques.
key_facts: Walter Doherty and Arvind Thadani published the ~400ms requirement in the IBM Systems Journal in 1982, replacing the prior 2-second standard.
Around 400ms is the last delay at which interaction still feels continuous rather than a wait.
Sub-400ms systems in the original research let users complete substantially more transactions per hour with higher satisfaction.
On the modern web, Google's INP metric sets a stricter bar of <=200ms at the 75th percentile for good responsiveness.
examples: Optimistic UI to stay under the threshold

sources.title: Laws of UX, Ch.10: Doherty Threshold

entries.name: Perceived performance
entries.summary: How fast an interface feels, which depends on feedback and expectation management as much as on actual measured speed.
entries.significance: Two systems with identical load times can feel very different; managing perception is often cheaper and more impactful than raw speed gains.
key_facts: Users perceive skeleton UIs as faster than spinners even when actual load times are identical.
Immediate feedback (within the ~400ms Doherty window) keeps attention on the task rather than the wait.
Showing what is happening and letting users anticipate content makes a system feel faster and users more patient.
Optimistic UI (showing the expected result before the server confirms) shortens perceived latency.

sources.title: Everything you need to know about skeleton screens

entries.name: Meaningful vs decorative motion
entries.summary: The principle that UI animation should serve a purpose, orienting, giving feedback, showing relationships, rather than decorating for its own sake.
entries.significance: Distinguishes motion that improves usability from motion that distracts or slows users; a core discipline of restraint in motion design.
key_facts: Functional motion clarifies cause/effect, spatial relationships, and hierarchy; decorative motion adds none of these.
Material frames motion as expressive but purposeful: it should focus attention and maintain continuity, not merely entertain.
Excessive or gratuitous animation increases cognitive load and can slow task completion.
Decorative, non-essential effects are the first thing to remove under prefers-reduced-motion.
examples: Transition showing where a closed panel went (functional)

sources.title: What Does Disney Know About Interface Animation Anyway?

entries.name: Reduced motion accessibility (prefers-reduced-motion)
entries.summary: A CSS media feature that detects a user's OS setting to minimize non-essential motion, letting sites tone down or remove animation for motion-sensitive users.
entries.significance: A required accessibility practice: motion can cause real physical harm (dizziness, nausea, migraine) for people with vestibular disorders.
key_facts: @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) detects the user's request to minimize non-essential motion.
Vestibular disorder reactions to motion include dizziness, nausea, and migraine; over 35% of adults experience vestibular dysfunction by age 40.
Best practice: remove decorative effects (parallax, large slides, autoplay) and replace essential animations with a static or minimal alternative.
WCAG 2.3.3 (Animation from Interactions, AAA) asks that motion triggered by interaction be disableable unless essential.
examples: Swapping a slide transition for a cross-fade under reduced motion

sources.title: prefers-reduced-motion: Sometimes less movement is more

entries.name: FLIP technique
entries.summary: A performance pattern (First, Last, Invert, Play) for animating layout changes using cheap transforms instead of expensive layout properties.
entries.significance: Lets developers animate position/size changes at 60fps by avoiding layout thrash, foundational to smooth layout and shared-element animations.
key_facts: FLIP stands for First (measure start), Last (measure end), Invert (transform back to start), Play (transition to identity); described by Paul Lewis.
It animates GPU-friendly opacity and transform instead of layout-triggering properties (width, height, top, left), avoiding reflow/repaint.
You compute the delta between start and end states, apply an inverting transform, then remove it under a transition to 'play'.
It underpins layout animations in libraries like Framer Motion and GSAP Flip.
examples: Animating a list reflow after reorder
Card smoothly expanding to a new position

sources.title: Animating Layouts with the FLIP Technique

entries.name: CSS scroll-driven animations
entries.summary: A CSS module that links an animation's progress to scroll position (scroll-timeline) or an element's visibility within its scroller (view-timeline), instead of clock time.
entries.significance: Brings performant, declarative scroll-linked motion to CSS, running off the main thread, replacing jank-prone JS scroll listeners.
key_facts: A scroll-progress timeline is tied to a scroll container's scroll position; a view-progress timeline tracks an element's position within its scroller.
The animation-timeline property assigns a scroll or view timeline to a CSS animation.
Because they run off the main thread, they avoid the jank of JS scroll/IntersectionObserver handlers.
Still honor prefers-reduced-motion, since scroll-linked motion can affect motion-sensitive users.
examples: Elements revealing as they enter view via view-timeline

sources.title: A guide to Scroll-driven Animations with just CSS
